description.task: Implement a probabilistic Bloom filter that can definitively say an element is NOT in the set, or probably IS in the set. Use a bit array with multiple hash functions. Demonstrate false positives.
instructions: mightContain: for(int i=0;i<numHashes;i++) if(!bits.get(hash(element,i))) return false;
Added elements (apple, banana, cherry) always return true (no false negatives — guaranteed property)
durian and elderberry will likely return false (not in filter)
A Bloom filter can have false positives (thinks item is there when it's not) but never false negatives
description.expectedOutput:
apple:   true
banana:  true
cherry:  true
durian:  false
elderberry: false
No false negatives: true

description.task: Implement a Count-Min Sketch, a probabilistic data structure for frequency estimation. It uses a 2D counter table with multiple hash functions. Overestimates are possible but never underestimates. Estimate element frequencies.
instructions: With width=20 and depth=4, estimates should be very accurate for small datasets
Count-Min Sketch may overestimate but never underestimate
Typical output: estimates equal true counts (for small stream with large width)
description.expectedOutput:
Estimated a: 5 (true=5)
Estimated b: 2 (true=2)
Estimated c: 2 (true=2)
Estimated d: 1 (true=1)
Never underestimates: true

description.task: Demonstrate practical performance of HashMap and HashSet for membership testing and counting. Compare O(1) hash lookup vs O(n) linear search for large datasets. Measure approximate relative lookup counts.
instructions: Hash lookup is O(1) while linear is O(n); for n=10000 the speedup should be significant
The timing confirms hash structures dramatically outperform linear search
The exact timing values will vary — only the relative comparison (hashTime < linearTime) matters
description.expectedOutput:
Linear search ms: 15
Hash lookup ms:   0
Hash is faster: true
Frequency: {1=2, 2=1, 3=2, 4=1, 5=3, 6=1, 9=1}
